title: Tree Removal for Rental Properties in Florida: Landlord, Tenant, and Storm-Damage Checklist
strengths: Strong tenant-reporting, owner authorization, emergency-versus-planned work, lease review, access, permits, insurance documentation, cleanup, and turnover coverage.
weaknesses: Very long; visible Internal Links to Add section; raw source URLs; overlaps the separate rental-damage article; legal statements about duties and access need tighter qualification; no direct service-page links.
ai_notes: Short Answer followed by many role, law, HOA, permit, insurance, access, scope, cleanup, stump, neighbor, prevention, red-flag, and checklist sections.
florida_notes: Useful Florida rental context, but Chapter 83, lease terms, property type, local rules, and emergency facts must remain distinct rather than implying one universal landlord-tree duty.
internal_link_opportunities: /services/emergency-response/, /services/tree-removal/, rental damage article, written estimate, storm cleanup, stump grinding.
cta_recommendation: Make this the authorization, access, vendor-scope, and records workflow; send casualty and lease-rights questions to the separate rental-damage page and legal counsel.
image_prompt: A Florida rental-home workflow showing tenant reports hazard, landlord or property manager authorizes work, utility and insurer checks, tenant access notice, tree crew scope, cleanup, stump decision, and final records.

title: Condo Association Tree Removal in Florida: Common Elements, Board Approval, and Storm Cleanup
strengths: Strong tree-location, declaration, common-element, board-approval, permit, resident-safety, contractor, debris, stump, and records workflow.
weaknesses: Excessive length; public editor note and raw sources; common-element maintenance, material alteration, and emergency authority are simplified; overlaps HOA and multifamily pages; no direct service links.
ai_notes: Ten-step checklist, scenario catalogue, records, red flags, board questions, CTA, disclaimer, sources, and FAQ structure.
florida_notes: Florida Statute 718.113 supports an association common-element maintenance role, subject to declaration-assigned limited-common-element duties; it does not decide every tree authorization or alteration question.
internal_link_opportunities: /services/tree-removal/, /services/emergency-response/, HOA page, multifamily page, written estimate, cleanup scope.
cta_recommendation: Center the page on location classification, declaration and board authority, resident closure plan, and written association scope.
image_prompt: A Florida condominium site plan labeling unit, common element, limited common element, parking, pool deck, sidewalk, right-of-way, preserve, board approval, resident closure, and tree-service work zone.

title: Commercial Property Tree Removal in Florida: Permits, Access, Liability, and Cleanup Checklist
strengths: Best-defined commercial intent in the group; strong ownership, site-plan, permits, pedestrian/vehicle control, utilities, contractor documents, operations, debris, stump, and recordkeeping coverage.
weaknesses: Still overlong; visible editor note and raw URLs; workers' compensation wording needs business-specific verification rather than a simplified rule; repeats apartment and association material; no commercial-specific service route.
ai_notes: Short Answer, exhaustive property types, sequential operational sections, red flags, editor notes, CTA, disclaimer, sources, and FAQ.
florida_notes: Correctly warns that the single-family definition in section 163.045 should not be assumed to cover ordinary commercial sites; local site-plan and landscape obligations need property-specific confirmation.
internal_link_opportunities: /services/tree-removal/, /services/emergency-response/, crane access, property protection, written estimate, cleanup and stump scope.
cta_recommendation: Make this the operating-site scope page: authority, business continuity, work-zone control, insurance documents, access, debris, and final reopening.
image_prompt: A Florida shopping center or office property tree-removal plan showing pedestrian detour, ADA route, blocked parking bays, fire lane, delivery access, utility area, equipment staging, debris zone, and reopening checklist.

title: HOA Tree Removal in Florida: What Homeowners and Boards Should Clarify First
strengths: Strong private-lot versus common-area, easement, preserve, right-of-way, documents, local permit, risk documentation, contractor, cleanup, stump, homeowner, and board questions.

title: Who Is Responsible When a Neighbor’s Tree Falls in Florida?
strengths: Strong safety-first, scene documentation, insurer-first practical step, prior-condition evidence, HOA location, unstable debris, and records guidance.
weaknesses: No H1, sources, internal links, or updated date; liability discussion is presented through an overly simple healthy-versus-hazardous distinction; statements about fault, notice, and payment need legal review and source support.
ai_notes: Short Answer, many what-if sections, better questions, final takeaway, phone CTA, and repetitive FAQs.
florida_notes: Responsibility is fact-, policy-, ownership-, negligence-, notice-, damage-, and jurisdiction-specific. The page should avoid deciding liability and instead guide safety, documentation, insurer contact, and attorney referral.
internal_link_opportunities: /services/emergency-response/, storm photos, tree touching power lines, HOA page, boundary tree guide, insurance preventive removal.
cta_recommendation: Tree service should be limited to stabilization and cleanup; insurer and Florida attorney should handle coverage and liability conclusions.
image_prompt: A Florida property-line tree fallen across two yards, with safety exclusion, utility check, photos, trunk origin, property damage, insurer calls, emergency stabilization, cleanup records, and legal-review path.

title: Tree Damage on Rental Properties: Who Handles What?
strengths: Excellent conceptual separation of structural repair, casualty-damage rights, and landlord-versus-tenant insurance losses.
weaknesses: No H1, sources, service links, CTA, or updated date; multiple unreplaced turn-based citation artifacts are live; legal conclusions are stated too broadly; repeats casualty points and lacks emergency tree-work workflow.
ai_notes: Cleaner prose than the checklist pages, but repeated distinctions and final takeaway remain; visible citation code is the dominant quality failure.
florida_notes: Sections 83.51, 83.53, and 83.63 are relevant, but their application can vary with property type, lease, code coverage, tenant conduct, damage facts, and legal procedure.
internal_link_opportunities: /services/emergency-response/, rental tree-removal checklist, storm documentation, tree on roof, insurer-preparation guide.
cta_recommendation: Add a safety and property-response CTA while clearly directing lease-rights disputes to qualified Florida legal counsel.
image_prompt: A Florida rental home struck by a tree with three clearly separated lanes: building repair and safety, tenant casualty and lease questions, and landlord versus renter insurance and personal-property records.
